reasoning: The finding is confirmed. Line 37 outputs the result of get_signup_url() inside a JavaScript string literal using esc_url_raw(), which does not escape double-quotes or backslashes and is therefore the wrong escape function for a JS string context. get_signup_url() applies the 'omnisend_signup_wp_link' filter and returns the filtered value unmodified when the naked domain equals 'omnisend.com' (lines 181-185). The domain guard only checks the host portion — a filter returning a URL like https://sub.omnisend.com/path?q="});alert(1)// would pass the guard and survive esc_url_raw() with the double-quote intact, breaking out of the JS string. No additional sanitizer exists between apply_filters and the echo. The auth gate is real: display() checks current_user_can('manage_options') at line 21, so the sink is admin-only. The attack requires a co-installed malicious (or compromised) plugin to hook 'omnisend_signup_wp_link' and return an omnisend.com-scoped URL containing JS-special characters; it is not directly user-controlled. If the domain check fails (non-omnisend.com host), the code falls back to the hardcoded static default via esc_url(), which is safe. Severity low is correct: real wrong-context escape, but practical exploitability requires a plugin-to-plugin attack chain and admin session.
